message:
fix(fcp-host/admin_state): cap not_before_delay_secs + monotonic token-verification clock

Two independent security-relevant tightenings on the capability-token
issuance and verification paths.

## 1. not_before_delay_secs now has a 10-year ceiling

CapabilityIssuanceRequest::not_before_delay_secs is a u64 that the
caller can set to anything from 0 to 18_446_744_073_709_551_615. The
issuance path was converting it to i64 seconds via
`i64::try_from(delay).unwrap_or(0)` which silently floor-clamped
overflows to zero — meaning a request with
not_before_delay_secs = u64::MAX produced a token that was
immediately valid (delay == 0) instead of failing closed. An
attacker-controlled caller could use this to sneak a "delayed
activation" token past an operator who thought the delay was in
effect.

Fix: define a hard cap

    const MAX_NOT_BEFORE_DELAY_SECS: u64 = 10 * 365 * 24 * 60 * 60;

(roughly 10 years — well beyond any legitimate deferred-activation
use case) and a validator

    fn validated_not_before_delay_secs(delay_secs: u64) -> Result<i64, LifecycleError>

that returns the i64 on success or a descriptive
LifecycleError::Persistence error when the value exceeds the cap or
the subsequent i64::try_from overflows. Wire the validator into the
issuance path with the standard .transpose()? shape so any Err
propagates correctly.

Doc comment on the field references MAX_NOT_BEFORE_DELAY_SECS so
callers can see the constant.

## 2. Token verification now uses a monotonic "effective now"

HostAdminStateStore::verify_capability_token previously read Utc::now()
inline on every verify call. That's fine if the wall clock only moves
forward, but a misbehaving NTP sync or a VM snapshot-restore can
move the clock BACKWARD by enough to revalidate tokens that were
already expired at the time of a previous verify call — the
decision would flip without any token state changing. Not
theoretically exploitable (the token is still cryptographically
signed) but the operator-facing "this token was rejected at 12:00"
promise can silently be violated.

Fix: persist `last_observed_time_ms: i64` on HostAdminStateSnapshot
and compute an `effective_now` for verification as
`max(observed_now_ms, last_observed_time_ms)`. That's a monotonic
clock bolted on top of Utc::now() — it can only move forward from
the highest value this store has ever seen. Any backward wall-clock
jump is absorbed by the floor.

The two public entry points
(inspect_capability_token + verify_capability_token) forward to new
`_at(token, now)` variants with an explicit DateTime parameter.
Production callers continue to pass Utc::now() via the old entry
points; tests can pass a fixture clock through the _at variants
for deterministic verification behavior across clock-dependent
scenarios.

HostAdminStateSnapshot serialization adds the field with
#[serde(default)] so existing snapshots on disk deserialize cleanly
into a snapshot with last_observed_time_ms=0 (which means the
first verify call bumps it to current wall-clock).

## Regression tests (two shown here; more in the suite)

  - issue_capability_token_rejects_overflowing_not_before_delay:
    request with not_before_delay_secs = u64::MAX; assert Err with
    message containing "not_before_delay_secs" and "exceeds max".

  - issue_capability_token_rejects_not_before_delay_above_cap:
    request with not_before_delay_secs = MAX + 1; asserts the same
    rejection shape. This is the just-above-the-cap edge that
    catches off-by-one regressions in the comparison direction.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
